FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=7514; ages 9-10 years at baseline; 2016-2018)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (hours per day) as primary exposure in Year 1; also measures problematic social media use (addictive scale) in Year 2

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression testing association between social media time (Year 1) and alcohol sipping (Year 3); mediation analysis with problematic social media use (Year 2) as mediator

FT_IC4.evidence: RR = 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43) for social media time predicting new-onset sipping; mediation: 25.0% reduction, p = 0.002

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis from ABCD Study with 3-year follow-up examining associations and mediation

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/social media as independent variable predicting non-SM outcome (alcohol sipping). Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (phone/SM must be dependent variable with only demographic predictors).
notes: Clear inclusion. Prospective association with social media time as IV and alcohol sipping as DV. Includes mediation analysis via problematic social media use scale.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=4,718 U.S. adolescents aged 10-15 years; 2018-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures generic 'recreational screen time' (hours per day, categorized as low/medium/high) with no smartphone or social media-specific breakdown. Paper does not isolate phone/SM variable as required.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT_IC3 evaluation dependent on FT_IC2 status. Since exposure does not meet FT_IC2 (not specific phone/SM), FT_IC3 cannot be met.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values exist (e.g., B = -2.85 for HDL cholesterol) but for ineligible generic screen time exposure, not smartphone/SM-specific.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis from ABCD Study examining joint associations of screen time and physical activity with CVD risk factors

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (generic screen time, not phone/SM-specific), making it ineligible regardless of FT-EC7 status.
notes: EXCLUDE via FT_IC2. Screen time is measured generically (recreational hours per day) without smartphone or social media-specific coefficients extractable. Does not meet narrowing criteria for smartphone/social-media-specific exposures.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=9,538 adolescents; 9-10 years at baseline 2016-2018; two-year follow-up)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple smartphone/social-media-specific screen types: video chat, texting, videos, video games. Abstract states 'The specific screen types with the greatest associations with depressive symptoms included video chat, texting, videos, and video games' with separately reported beta coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with inferential tests linking screen time to mental health outcomes (depression, conduct, somatic, ADHD symptoms). Examples: depressive B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06-0.13, p<0.001; conduct B=0.07, 95% CI 0.03-0.10, p<0.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple beta coefficients reported: depressive (B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06-0.13, p<0.001); conduct (B=0.07, 95% CI 0.03-0.10); somatic (B=0.06, 95% CI 0.01-0.11, p=0.026); ADHD (B=0.06, 95% CI 0.01-0.10, p=0.013)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis from ABCD Study with two-year longitudinal follow-up examining baseline screen time predicting mental health outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable predicting mental health outcomes (dependent variables), not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 only applies when phone/SM is DV.
notes: Clear inclusion. Prospective analysis with smartphone/SM-specific screen types (video chat, texting, videos, video games) as IVs predicting mental health outcomes. Coefficients separately reported for specific screen types.
